title: Linguine al granchio
subtitle: Brown and white crab, lemon, and chilli over flat noodles
excerpt: Fresh crab tossed through linguine with garlic, lemon, and chilli. Both the white meat and the brown go in: the white for texture, the brown for depth. This is a summer dish from the Ligurian coast, simple enough to respect good crab and fast enough not to overcook it.
sourceNotes: Linguine with crab is a classic of the Ligurian and Venetian coasts, appearing in regional Italian cooking literature throughout the twentieth century. The technique of using brown crab meat to build the sauce and white crab to finish is documented in Elizabeth David, Italian Food (1954), which draws on Italian regional tradition. A related preparation appears in Artusi's chapters on coastal cookery (La scienza in cucina, 1891, public domain).

content.text: This recipe uses both parts of the crab deliberately. The brown meat, which is intensely flavoured and slightly grainy, goes into the pan with the garlic and oil to form the sauce base. The white meat, delicate and sweet, goes in at the very end, barely warmed, not cooked. Handling them together in the same pan from the start turns the white meat mushy and dulls its flavour.

A dressed crab from a fishmonger gives you both meats separated and ready. Whole cooked crabs can be picked yourself, but the yield per crab is often disappointing, count on needing two medium crabs per person for a main course.

items.name: Crab meat
items.prepNote: mixed white and brown, roughly 200 g white and 100 g brown

items.name: Red chilli
items.prepNote: finely sliced, seeds removed if you prefer less heat

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: zest of one, juice of both

content.text: Bring a large pan of well-salted water to a boil. Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of linguine until 2 minutes short of al dente. Scoop out 150 ml of pasta water. Drain.

While the pasta cooks, warm the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the red chilli. Cook for 1 to 2 minutes until the garlic is pale gold. Add the brown crab meat and stir it in, breaking it up slightly. Cook for 1 minute. Add 100 ml of the pasta water and the lemon zest. Let it simmer for 1 minute until slightly saucy.

Add the drained linguine to the pan and toss over medium heat for 1 minute. Take the pan off the heat. Add the white crab meat, the lemon juice, and the parsley. Toss gently. You want the white crab in recognisable flakes, not broken up. Add a grind of black pepper and serve immediately.

items.fix: Add white crab off the heat, after the pan is removed from the flame
items.cause: Added too early and cooked through rather than barely warmed
items.symptom: White crab meat is mushy and tasteless

items.fix: Keep pasta water close; add in 2-tablespoon splashes and toss until glossy
items.cause: Not enough pasta water, or pasta absorbed all the liquid before the crab went in

items.fix: The brown meat is essential for depth; do not leave it out
items.cause: Brown crab meat not used, or it was very mild and fresh from a tank rather than a caught crab

items.fix: Add juice in stages, tasting as you go; some lemons need only one
items.cause: Too much lemon juice, or the lemon was very acidic

content.text: Linguine al granchio appears on menus from Genoa to Venice, with variations along the way. The Ligurian version tends to be simpler, closer to the one here. The Venetian version often includes a splash of cream or brandy. British cooks have adopted it enthusiastically, partly because brown crab (brown or spider crab from the West Country or Scotland) is so well suited to it. The dish is a good argument for buying a whole dressed crab from a fishmonger rather than the pasteurised prepared crab in plastic tubs, which lacks the brown meat entirely.
